content.text:
```json
{
  "signature": "This eighteen-bar theme is a model of Classical symmetry: the A section (bars 1–8) establishes A major with quiet confidence, moving from tonic through a mediant colour at bar 3 and a secondary dominant (V/V) before a full perfect authentic cadence closes the section at bar 8. The B section then gently unsettles that stability — a half cadence on the dominant at bar 12 leaves the music suspended, after which Mozart tonicizes E major from bar 13, treating it as a temporary home with its own perfect authentic cadence at bar 16, before a concise two-bar coda (bars 17–18) steers the harmony back to A major with a final V7–I.",
  "significance": "That brief visit to E major acts like a short detour on a familiar walk: everything around it feels reassuringly recognizable, yet the moment of arriving somewhere slightly different and then finding your way back home gives the theme a gentle sense of longing and resolution that makes it extraordinarily singable and memorable.",
  "connection": "Bach's Fugue No. 19 in A major from the Well-Tempered Clavier shares the same home key and, more deeply, the same instinct to treat A major not as a fixed point but as something to be briefly left and joyfully recovered. Where Mozart uses a clean tonicization of E major to create that away-and-home arc, Bach typically achieves a similar effect through the contrapuntal weaving of his subject into related keys — most likely E major and F-sharp minor — before the final entries draw everything back. Both composers seem to understand that A major, for all its brightness, earns its warmth only when the listener has been allowed, for a moment, to feel its absence."
}
```
